clue: Henry VIII's second wife, she was executed for treason in 1536, with the circumstances of her marriage regarded as a key catalyst for the English Reformation.
proposed_clue: This second wife of Henry VIII was executed for treason in 1536; Henry's pursuit of their marriage, which required a break from Rome, helped spark the English Reformation.
why: Original opens with a possessive subordinate phrase and no 'this [noun]' marker; the passive 'with the circumstances of her marriage regarded as a key catalyst' is loose and wordy.

clue: The final emperor of the Julio-Claudian dynasty, this ruler reigned from 54 to 68 AD before dying by suicide; his mother, the influential Agrippina the Younger, was murdered on his orders.
proposed_clue: This ruler reigned from 54 to 68 AD as the last Julio-Claudian emperor, dying by suicide — and had his own mother, Agrippina the Younger, murdered on his orders.
why: Leading appositive delays 'this ruler'; 'final emperor of the Julio-Claudian dynasty' and the passive Agrippina clause both compress cleanly.

clue: This author devised the Three Laws of Robotics across his science-fiction stories.
proposed_clue: This author devised the Three Laws of Robotics.
why: 'across his science-fiction stories' re-states context already implied by 'Three Laws of Robotics'

clue: Count Vronsky's affair with this Tolstoy title heroine begins and ends with a death at a train station.
proposed_clue: This Tolstoy title heroine's affair with Count Vronsky begins and ends with a death at a train station.
why: 'Count Vronsky's affair with this Tolstoy title heroine' buries the answer in a subordinate possessive; restructured to lead with 'This … heroine'

clue: In this author's novels, 42 is the Answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything.
proposed_clue: This author's novels declare 42 to be the Answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything.
why: Leading prepositional phrase 'In this author's novels' buries the answer; restructured so 'this author' governs the sentence

clue: The greatest novelist of the Victorian era by many accounts, this English author left school at 12 when his father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a boot-blacking factory.
proposed_clue: This Victorian author of 'Oliver Twist' and 'Great Expectations' left school at 12 when his father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a boot-blacking factory.
lessons: Lead with a vivid, concrete hook
why: Opening superlative 'The greatest novelist … by many accounts' is abstract and buries the subject; leading with specific works gives a vivid hook and fixes both

clue: Agatha Christie's most famous and longest-running fictional creation, this fastidious Belgian detective relies on his 'little grey cells' and appears in 33 novels and 51 short stories.
proposed_clue: This fastidious Belgian detective relies on his 'little grey cells' to solve cases across 33 Agatha Christie novels and 51 short stories.
why: 'Agatha Christie's most famous and longest-running fictional creation' is an abstract rank that buries 'this … detective'; restructured to lead with the answer

clue: An example of the literary nonsense genre, this 1865 Lewis Carroll children's novel follows a young girl who tumbles down a rabbit hole into a fantasy world of anthropomorphic creatures.
proposed_clue: This 1865 Lewis Carroll children's novel follows a young girl who tumbles down a rabbit hole into a fantasy world of anthropomorphic creatures.
lessons: Lead with a vivid, concrete hook
why: Opening 'An example of the literary nonsense genre' is an abstract category label that delays the subject; cutting it lets the concrete plot detail lead

clue: Though he saw himself primarily as a poet, this English Victorian author achieved fame through his novels set in the fictional county of Wessex, including 'Tess of the d'Urbervilles.'
proposed_clue: This English Victorian author set his novels in the fictional county of Wessex — including 'Tess of the d'Urbervilles' — though he considered himself primarily a poet.
why: Leading subordinate clause 'Though he saw himself primarily as a poet' buries 'this English Victorian author'; restructured to lead with the subject

clue: The American novelist who coined the term 'Jazz Age' in a 1922 short story collection achieved transient success in the 1920s but received critical acclaim only after his death.
proposed_clue: This American novelist coined the term 'Jazz Age' and wrote 'The Great Gatsby,' though critical acclaim came only after his death.
lessons: Lead with a vivid, concrete hook
why: Uses a relative clause ('The American novelist who…') with no 'this' to mark the answer, buries the subject, and omits his most famous work as a concrete hook

clue: Concern over this company's bankruptcy centered on the genetic data stored in its databases.
proposed_clue: Concern over this company's bankruptcy centered on its customers' genetic data.
why: 'stored in its databases' is redundant filler; 'its customers' genetic data' is tighter and more precise.

clue: In 'Top Gun,' Maverick starts a rivalry with this rival pilot played by Val Kilmer.
proposed_clue: In 'Top Gun,' Maverick clashes with this pilot played by Val Kilmer.
why: 'starts a rivalry with this rival pilot' repeats the same concept twice; 'clashes with this pilot' eliminates the redundancy.

clue: This Greek philosopher held that 'eudaimonia,' or flourishing, is the highest human good.
proposed_clue: Author of the 'Nicomachean Ethics,' this ancient thinker held that 'eudaimonia' — human flourishing — is the highest good.
lessons: Lead with vivid/concrete hook — not category labels
why: 'This Greek philosopher' is a generic category label; leading with a specific work gives a concrete anchor.

clue: This ethical stance prioritizes actions that benefit the long-run future and far-future generations.
proposed_clue: This ethical stance holds that improving the lives of people in the far future should be humanity's top priority.
why: 'long-run' echoes the answer word, and 'long-run future and far-future generations' is redundant.

clue: Romanized from the Greek Nike during the First Punic War, she came to symbolize Rome's hegemony and right to rule; a deified abstraction with virtually no mythology of her own, she left behind a name now borne by far more women than worshippers.
proposed_clue: Romanized from the Greek Nike during the First Punic War, she symbolized Rome's imperial hegemony; a deified abstraction with virtually no mythology, she left behind a name borne by far more women than worshippers.
why: 'Came to symbolize,' 'hegemony and right to rule,' and 'of her own' add words without adding meaning.

clue: This constant, approximately 6.022 x 10^23, is how many particles are contained in one mole of a substance.
proposed_clue: This constant, approximately 6.022 × 10^23, counts the particles in one mole of a substance.
why: "is how many particles are contained in" is passive and wordy; "counts the particles in" carries the same meaning in four fewer words.

clue: The wings of these tiny birds can beat up to about 80 times per second.
proposed_clue: The wings of these tiny birds beat up to 80 times per second.
why: "can beat up to about" stacks two hedges ("can" + "about"); dropping both tightens without changing the stated fact.

clue: Paul, a member of this eight-armed species, correctly called all eight matches he was given at the 2010 World Cup.
proposed_clue: Paul, a member of this eight-armed species, correctly predicted all eight of his 2010 World Cup picks.
why: "correctly called all eight matches he was given at" is passive-weak; rephrasing removes the trailing passive clause without losing any fact.

clue: Native to much of South America, it is the largest living rodent.
proposed_clue: Native to South America, this animal is the largest living rodent.
why: "it is" leaves the answer referent unmarked; swapping to "this animal is" flags the subject clearly, and "much of" is vague filler that can drop.

clue: Produced by certain molluscs to line the inside of their shells, this iridescent material is the same substance that forms a pearl.
proposed_clue: Produced by certain molluscs to line their shells, this iridescent material also forms pearls.
why: "the inside of their shells" and "is the same substance that forms a pearl" are both wordy; cutting to "their shells" and "also forms pearls" drops ~8 words without losing any fact.

clue: This biome of conifers — pines, spruces, and larches — is the world’s largest land biome, ringing the subarctic across Canada, Scandinavia, and Russia.
proposed_clue: The world's largest land biome, this belt of conifers — pines, spruces, and larches — rings the subarctic across Canada, Scandinavia, and Russia.
why: "biome" appears twice in the original; restructuring leads with the superlative as a hook and eliminates the repetition.

clue: This Hall of Famer stole a record 1,406 bases, far more than any other player in MLB history.
proposed_clue: This Hall of Famer stole 1,406 career bases, the MLB record.
why: 'A record' and 'far more than any other player in MLB history' say the same thing; cutting the trailing clause removes pure redundancy.

clue: This Kenner toy introduced in 1963 originally used a pair of ordinary incandescent light bulbs as its heat source; Kenner sold 500,000 units in the first year of production.
proposed_clue: Introduced by Kenner in 1963, this toy used a pair of incandescent light bulbs as its heat source and sold 500,000 units in the first year.
why: 'Kenner' appears twice and 'ordinary' adds nothing; restructuring removes both redundancies and converts the semicolon break into a tighter compound predicate.

clue: This line of collectible plush toys, created by Hong Kong illustrator Kasing Lung and sold exclusively by Pop Mart in sealed 'blind box' packaging that hides which figure is inside, became a global phenomenon in 2024.
proposed_clue: This line of collectible plush toys, created by Hong Kong illustrator Kasing Lung and sold by Pop Mart in sealed 'blind box' packaging, became a global phenomenon in 2024.
why: 'That hides which figure is inside' restates what 'blind box' already means; removing it cuts eight words without losing any information.

clue: In this winter Olympic sport, teams of four players take turns sliding polished granite stones toward a circular target — called 'the house' — on a sheet of ice, while two teammates sweep with brooms to guide the stone's path.
proposed_clue: In this winter Olympic sport, four-player teams take turns sliding polished granite stones toward a circular target — the house — on a sheet of ice, while two teammates sweep with brooms to guide the stone's path.
why: 'Teams of four players' compresses to 'four-player teams' and '— called the house —' to '— the house —', cutting four words with no information loss.

clue: Borrowed directly from French, this word names a listless weariness and dissatisfaction that arises from boredom or a lack of excitement.
proposed_clue: Borrowed directly from French, this word names a listless weariness and dissatisfaction arising from boredom.
why: 'Or a lack of excitement' is redundant with 'boredom'; 'that arises from' compresses to 'arising from'.

clue: The surname of an 18th-century German physician who claimed to heal patients through 'animal magnetism' is the origin of this word, meaning 'to hold someone so spellbound that they seem entranced.'
proposed_clue: This verb, meaning to hold someone so spellbound they seem entranced, comes from an 18th-century German physician who claimed to heal patients through 'animal magnetism.'
why: Original buries 'this word' at the end of a long nominal subject clause; restructuring puts the subject marker first and drops the filler 'that'.

clue: Coined by the writer Sylvia Wright in 1954, this term for a misheard word or phrase — most often a misinterpreted song lyric — arose when she reinterpreted a ballad's line 'laid him on the green' as the name of a slain noblewoman.
proposed_clue: This term for a misheard word or phrase — most often a song lyric — was coined by writer Sylvia Wright in 1954, who named it after mishearing a line of a Scottish ballad as the name of a slain noblewoman.
lessons: Lead with a vivid hook — not provenance
why: The quoted phrase 'laid him on the green' contains '-green,' a phonetic tail of the answer; restructured to front 'this term' and removed the leak while preserving the origin story.
